---
title: "Lesson 5: General Confidence Intervals 2"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---


::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_general-confidence-intervals-2.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_general-confidence-intervals-2.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-general-confidence-intervals-2_module5_lesson5.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## General Confidence Intervals: Using the Sample Minimum {#sec-general-ci-sample-minimum}

Suppose 
$X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{Exp}(\lambda) \qquad \lambda>0$
Construct a 95% confidence interval for $\lambda$ based on the minimum observation in the sample.

### Distribution of the Sample Minimum {#sec-distribution-sample-minimum}

Define
$Y_n=\min(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)$

For each $X_i$, the cumulative distribution function is

$$
F_X(x) = P(X_i\leq x) = 1-e^{-\lambda x} \qquad x\geq 0
$$

The cumulative distribution function of $Y_n$ is

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{Y_n}(y)
&=P(Y_n\leq y) \\
&= P\!\left(\min(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)\leq y\right) \\
&= 1-P\left(\min(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)>y\right) \\
&= 1-P(X_1>y,X_2>y,\ldots,X_n>y) \\
&= 1-\prod_{i=1}^{n}P(X_i>y)&& \text{independence} \\
&= 1-\left[P(X_1>y)\right]^n && \text{identical distributions} \\
&= 1-\left[1-F_X(y)\right]^n \\
&= 1-\left[e^{-\lambda y}\right]^n \\
&= 1-e^{-n\lambda y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating gives the density

$$
f_{Y_n}(y) = \frac{d}{dy}F_{Y_n}(y) = n\lambda e^{-n\lambda y} \qquad y\geq 0
$$

::: {#thm-minimum-exponentials}
## Minimum of Independent Exponential Random Variables

The minimum of $n$ independent and identically distributed exponential random variables with rate $\lambda$ is exponential with rate $n\lambda$:

$$
\boxed{ Y_n \sim \mathcal{Exp}(n\lambda) }
$$
:::

### Step One: Choose a Statistic {#sec-minimum-ci-step-one}

Use
$Y_n=\min(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)$

### Step Two: Construct a Parameter-Free Pivot {#sec-minimum-ci-step-two}

Since

$$
Y_n
\sim
\mathcal{Exp}(n\lambda)
=
\Gamma(1,n\lambda)
$$

scaling gives

$$
\boxed{
n\lambda Y_n
\sim
\Gamma(1,1)
=
\mathcal{Exp}(1)
}
$$

### Step Three: Choose a 95% Probability Region {#sec-minimum-ci-step-three}

Let $W\sim\mathcal{Exp}(1)$ Choose $c$ so that
$P(0<W<c)=0.95$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^c 0.95 \\
1-e^{-c}&=0.95 \\
e^{-c}&=0.05 \\
c&=-\ln(0.05)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
P\!\left(0<n\lambda Y_n<-\ln(0.05)\right)=0.95
$$

### Step Four: Solve for the Rate {#sec-minimum-ci-step-four}

Because $nY_n>0$,

$$
0<\lambda<\frac{-\ln(0.05)}{nY_n}
$$

where

$$
Y_n
=
\min(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)
$$

::: {#def-one-sided-ci-exponential-minimum}
## One-Sided Confidence Interval Based on the Minimum

A one-sided $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\lambda$ is

$$
\boxed{\left(0,\frac{-\ln(\alpha)}{nY_n}\right)}
$$

For 95% confidence, set $\alpha=0.05$
:::


## Reflection
